questions.q: In which Indian city is the Taj Mahal located?
questions.explanation: The Taj Mahal stands on the south bank of the Yamuna River in Agra, Uttar Pradesh.

questions.q: Which Mughal emperor built the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: Emperor Shah Jahan commissioned the Taj Mahal as a mausoleum for his wife.

questions.q: For whom was the Taj Mahal built?
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan built the Taj Mahal as a tomb for his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal.

questions.q: What is the primary building material of the Taj Mahal's main structure?
questions.explanation: The Taj Mahal's central mausoleum is built of gleaming white marble.

questions.q: What type of building is the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: The Taj Mahal is a mausoleum, built to hold the tomb of Mumtaz Mahal.

questions.q: Beside which river does the Taj Mahal stand?
questions.explanation: The Taj Mahal is situated on the bank of the Yamuna River in Agra.

questions.q: The Taj Mahal is widely regarded as a symbol of what?
questions.explanation: Built by Shah Jahan in memory of his wife, the Taj Mahal is celebrated as a monument to love.

questions.q: The Taj Mahal is featured on which prestigious international list?
questions.explanation: The Taj Mahal was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1983.

questions.q: What crowns the central chamber of the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: A large bulbous white marble dome (the onion dome) crowns the central mausoleum.

questions.q: How many tall towers (minarets) stand at the corners of the Taj Mahal's plinth?
questions.explanation: Four minarets frame the marble platform, one at each corner of the plinth.

questions.q: In which Indian state is the Taj Mahal located?
questions.explanation: Agra, the home of the Taj Mahal, lies in the state of Uttar Pradesh.

questions.q: Construction of the Taj Mahal began in which century?
questions.explanation: Construction began around 1632 in the 17th century, after Mumtaz Mahal's death in 1631.

questions.q: To which architectural style does the Taj Mahal belong?
questions.explanation: The Taj Mahal is the supreme example of Mughal architecture, blending Persian, Islamic and Indian styles.

questions.q: The Taj Mahal was completed in approximately which year?
questions.explanation: The Taj Mahal complex was largely completed around 1653, roughly two decades after work began.

questions.q: What decorative technique uses inlaid semi-precious stones in patterns on the Taj Mahal's marble?
questions.explanation: The Taj Mahal is famed for pietra dura, inlay of semi-precious stones forming floral and calligraphic designs.

questions.q: What is inscribed in elegant calligraphy on the great gateway and arches of the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: Passages from the Quran are inlaid in black marble calligraphy on the entrance and facades.

questions.q: What lies symmetrically in front of the mausoleum, mirroring its image?
options: A large reflecting pool and garden
questions.explanation: A long reflecting watercourse runs through the charbagh garden, mirroring the marble facade.

questions.q: The formal garden layout of the Taj Mahal follows which classic Persian design?
questions.explanation: The grounds use the charbagh, a quartered Persian garden divided by walkways and water channels.

questions.q: A red sandstone mosque stands on which side of the Taj Mahal mausoleum?
questions.explanation: The mosque sits to the west of the mausoleum, facing Mecca, balanced by a matching jawab on the east.

questions.q: The building on the east side that mirrors the mosque (but is not used for prayer) is called the:
questions.explanation: The jawab ('answer') mirrors the mosque to preserve the complex's perfect symmetry.

questions.q: Besides Mumtaz Mahal, whose tomb was later placed inside the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan was later entombed beside Mumtaz Mahal, the only element that breaks the building's symmetry.

questions.q: Why are the four minarets of the Taj Mahal built tilting slightly outward?
options: So they would fall away from the tomb in an earthquake
questions.explanation: The minarets lean slightly outward so that, if they collapsed, they would fall away from the central tomb.

questions.q: In which year was Mumtaz Mahal's death, which prompted the building of the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: Mumtaz Mahal died in 1631 while giving birth to her fourteenth child, prompting Shah Jahan's grief and the monument.

questions.q: Which architect is most often credited as the chief designer of the Taj Mahal?
questions.explanation: Ustad Ahmad Lahauri is generally regarded as the principal architect of the Taj Mahal.

questions.q: From where did the white marble used to build the Taj Mahal mainly come?
questions.explanation: The marble was quarried at Makrana in Rajasthan and transported to Agra for the construction.

questions.q: What event brought Shah Jahan's reign to a close while the Taj Mahal era continued?
options: He was deposed and imprisoned by his son Aurangzeb
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan was deposed by his son Aurangzeb and held captive in Agra Fort until his death in 1666.

questions.q: From his confinement, Shah Jahan is said to have viewed the Taj Mahal from which place?
questions.explanation: Imprisoned by Aurangzeb in Agra Fort, Shah Jahan could see the distant Taj Mahal across the Yamuna.

questions.q: Which large red sandstone gateway forms the grand entrance to the Taj Mahal complex?
questions.explanation: The Darwaza-i Rauza, the monumental red sandstone Great Gate, serves as the main entrance to the complex.

questions.q: What environmental threat has notably contributed to yellowing and discoloration of the Taj Mahal's marble?
questions.explanation: Air pollution and acid rain in the Agra region have stained the marble, prompting protective measures like the Taj Trapezium Zone.

questions.q: Atop the main dome of the Taj Mahal sits a finial that combines Islamic and Hindu motifs; which feature does it include?
options: A crescent moon with horns shaped like a trident
questions.explanation: The gilded finial bears a crescent moon whose upturned horns evoke a trident, blending Islamic and Indian symbolism.
